teaching_card.title: Two-Way Tables (Easy)
teaching_card.body: A two-way table sorts data by two categories. Probabilities come from the right total: use a row or column total for that group, and the grand total for the whole sample.
key_points: Use the grand total for a whole-sample probability.

screens.title: Rows, columns, totals
screens.body: A two-way table sorts data by two categories at once. Each cell is a count; the row totals, column totals, and grand total sit on the edges. The whole question is choosing the right total to divide by.
key_points: Cells are counts; the margins are the totals.
Pick the total that matches what's being asked.

screens.title: Three kinds of probability
screens.body: Marginal: a whole row or column ÷ grand total. Joint: a single cell ÷ grand total ('both A and B'). Conditional: a cell ÷ that row's or column's total ('given A, the chance of B').
key_points: Marginal & joint divide by the grand total.
Conditional divides by the row/column you're given.

screens.title: 'Given' changes the denominator
screens.body: The word 'given' (or 'of the …') means you only look within that group, so divide by that group's total, not the grand total. This is the most common place to slip.
key_points: No 'given' -> denominator is the grand total.

screens.title: Try the moves
screens.body: Suppose 20 of 50 responses are in the Cats row, and within the Weekday column (total 30) 12 are Cats. P(Cats) = 20/50 = 2/5. P(Cats given Weekday) = 12/30 = 2/5 — note the different denominator.
